exercises.title: Security Level Comparison
exercises.description: Compare ML-DSA security levels with classical signature algorithms.
tasks: Create a comparison table of ML-DSA-44/65/87 vs RSA-2048/3072 and ECDSA P-256/384
Include classical bits, quantum bits, and signature/key sizes
Calculate security-per-byte ratio for each algorithm
Analyze which ML-DSA variant provides equivalent security to Ed25519 (classically)
Recommend algorithms for 10-year, 30-year, and 50-year validity requirements
exercises.expectedOutput: Comprehensive security comparison document with recommendations
hints: RSA-2048: ~112 classical bits, broken by quantum
ECDSA P-256: ~128 classical bits, broken by quantum

exercises.title: Side-Channel Attack Simulation
exercises.description: Demonstrate timing side-channel from rejection sampling and mitigation.
tasks: Implement simplified ML-DSA signing with rejection sampling
Measure iteration count for many signatures with fixed secret
Show correlation between iteration count and secret values
Implement mitigation: constant-iteration signing with dummy operations
Verify mitigation eliminates timing correlation
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np
import time

def sign_vulnerable(s: int, gamma1: int, beta: int) -> tuple:
    """
    Vulnerable signing that leaks iteration count.
    Returns (signature, iterations).
    """
    iterations = 0
    while True:
        iterations += 1
        y = np.random.randint(-gamma1 + 1, gamma1 + 1)
        c = np.random.randint(1, 10)  # Simplified challenge
        z = y + c * s
        
        if abs(z) < gamma1 - beta:
            return (c, z), iterations

def sign_constant_time(s: int, gamma1: int, beta: int, max_iter: int = 10) -> tuple:
    """
    Constant-time signing that always takes max_iter iterations.
    Returns signature without leaking iteration count.
    """
    result = None
    for i in range(max_iter):
        y = np.random.randint(-gamma1 + 1, gamma1 + 1)
        c = np.random.randint(1, 10)
        z = y + c * s
        
        # Your implementation: update result only on success
        # but always complete all iterations
        pass
    
    return result

# Analyze vulnerable implementation
gamma1, beta = 100, 20
for s in [1, 5, 10, 15]:
    iterations = [sign_vulnerable(s, gamma1, beta)[1] for _ in range(1000)]
    print(f"s={s}: mean_iterations={np.mean(iterations):.2f}")
exercises.expectedOutput: Demonstration showing timing leak and effective mitigation
hints: Larger s values may cause more rejections
Use constant_time_select to update result without branching
Compare timing distributions before and after mitigation

exercises.title: Forgery Attempt Analysis
exercises.description: Analyze why direct forgery attempts fail for ML-DSA.
tasks: Describe what an attacker needs to produce for a valid forgery
Explain why guessing z randomly fails (calculate probability)
Show why the MSIS problem prevents direct solving for z
Calculate the expected number of attempts for brute-force forgery
Compare with the security level claims
exercises.expectedOutput: Detailed analysis of forgery attack complexity
hints: Forger needs: valid (z, h) such that verification passes
z must satisfy: ||z||∞ < γ₁ - β AND challenge matches
Probability of random z satisfying both: ~2^{-256}

exercises.title: Implementation Security Audit
exercises.description: Create a security checklist and audit framework for ML-DSA implementations.
tasks: List all constant-time operation requirements
Identify all places where secrets must be protected
Create test cases for rejection sampling correctness
Design tests for detecting timing side-channels
Document memory handling requirements
exercises.expectedOutput: Comprehensive security audit checklist and test framework
hints: Focus on: NTT, polynomial operations, comparisons, branches
Secrets: s₁, s₂, K, intermediate values during signing
Timing tests: measure with different secrets, check variance
